target.title: fix(state): refuse chmod-less agent database volumes that cannot prove credential privacy
target.body:
## Summary

The per-agent SQLite database stores credentials (API keys and OAuth tokens under `agents/<agentId>/agent/`). On filesystems that reject `chmod` (Azure Files, NFS without `no_root_squash`, some Docker/SMB volume drivers), `ensureOpenClawAgentDatabasePermissions` called raw `chmodSync` and the agent could not open its database at all: it threw an uncaught `ENOTSUP`/`EPERM` before the handle opened, while the non-secret shared state DB came up fine.

This is the follow-up to #92786, which closed that gap by reusing the shared state DB helper `bestEffortChmodSync`. @vincentkoc closed #92786 because that helper is too permissive for a secret store: it would silently open a world-readable credential store on a chmod-less volume. The canonical paths named were an ACL-aware Windows verify, or a scoped policy that refuses unsafe agent DB locations. This PR implements the scoped fail-closed policy; the shared non-secret state DB is untouched and keeps its best-effort behavior.

## Fix

New `enforcePrivateAgentDbChmodSync` used for the agent DB directory and sidecar files, replacing the raw `chmodSync` calls. It proves the resulting mode is private after every `chmod` attempt, on both the success and the failure path, before the credential store is trusted:

- POSIX: after attempting `chmod`, OpenClaw reads the resulting mode with `stat` regardless of whether `chmod` reported success or threw. The store is tolerated only when `stat` proves it private (`mode & 0o077 === 0`) for the directory and each existing DB/WAL/SHM target; otherwise it refuses with a scoped, actionable error pointing the operator at a local path via `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. When `chmod` failed but the target is already private the chmod was cosmetic, so OpenClaw warns once and continues.
- Windows (`process.platform === "win32"`): refuse when `chmod` fails. Windows file modes do not reflect NTFS ACLs, so a failed `chmod` on a network-backed/SMB volume gives no proof the credential store is private.

This closes the gap raised in review: a volume can report a successful `chmod` while leaving the mode unchanged. Core CIFS mounted without the Unix extensions documents exactly this; `man mount.cifs` states that attempting to change permissions "will return success but have no effect", and `stat` then reports the mount `file_mode`/`dir_mode` (commonly `0644`/`0755`). Silent-ignore FUSE/bindfs mounts behave the same way. The previous helper only checked the resulting mode inside the `catch` path, so a silent-success volume sailed past the check and opened a `0644` credential DB. Verifying after every attempt refuses that case.

This is deliberately stricter than the sibling `bestEffortChmodSync`, which tolerates `ENOTSUP`/`EOPNOTSUPP`/`EINVAL` unconditionally for the non-secret store. For a credential store the only safe question is whether the secret is private now.

Every secret-bearing open path shares one gate. The read-only auth-profile loader (`readAuthProfileJsonCellReadOnly` in `src/agents/auth-profiles/sqlite.ts`, reached from `loadPersistedAuthProfileStore` -> `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`) previously opened the same `openclaw-agent.sqlite` with `DatabaseSync(..., { readOnly: true })` with no privacy check, so an existing `0644` credential DB from a chmod-less/silent-success volume was still read and trusted at auth-resolution time. Both the read-write open path and the read-only loader now run the existing DB/WAL/SHM files through one exported helper `enforcePrivateAgentDbFilePermissions`, so a store that cannot be proven private is refused before it is read, not only before it is written.

The post-open permission pass runs inside the same cleanup as schema setup. The refusal can fire on the second (post-open) pass when the agent directory is private but the freshly created DB file is born group/world readable on a chmod-less mount; keeping it inside the cleanup closes the live SQLite handle and WAL maintenance before the error escapes instead of leaking them. This PR is rebased on `main`; the open path now uses the `configureSqliteConnectionPragmas` cleanup added in ea346f4361, with the post-open permission pass moved inside that same `try` so a privacy refusal still releases the handle.

## Verification

- Colocated regression suite `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts` (9 cases): chmod success hardens to `0700`/`0600`; chmod fails but store already private opens; chmod reports success but the store stays world-readable refuses (the silent-ignore case); chmod fails on a non-private dir refuses; `win32` chmod failure refuses; `EACCES` on a non-private dir refuses; a post-open refusal closes the freshly opened handle (asserts every captured `DatabaseSync` instance reports `!isOpen`); a private store still loads through the read-only auth-profile path; and a silent-success world-readable store refuses on that same read-only path. The post-open case forces the target non-private through the resolved DB path so it is deterministic regardless of the ambient umask. Run with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts` -> 9 passed.
- `oxlint` on the changed files -> clean.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opening the per-agent credential database on a volume that does not apply `chmod` now opens only when the store is provably private, and otherwise refuses with a scoped error instead of silently storing credentials with permissive modes. This includes volumes that report a successful `chmod` while leaving the mode unchanged, which previously opened a world-readable credential DB. A refusal that fires after the database handle is already open closes that handle and its WAL maintenance instead of leaking them. The read-only auth-profile load path (`loadPersistedAuthProfileStore` -> `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`) shares the same gate, so an existing `0644` credential store is refused before it is read, not just before it is written.

Real environment tested: Linux (Node 22.19) running the production entry point `openOpenClawAgentDatabase` against a real temporary state dir. The chmod-less volume is reproduced at the OS layer with an LD_PRELOAD shim over libc chmod(2)/fchmod(2)/fchmodat(2): with `CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT` the calls return success without changing the mode (the documented CIFS-without-unix-extensions and silent-ignore FUSE behavior); with `ENOTSUP`/`EACCES` they return the matching real errno from the kernel call boundary. Store privacy is varied with the process umask (077 produces a private 0700/0600 store; 022 produces a group/world readable 0644 DB file that only becomes visible to the post-open permission pass). After each run the resulting agent DB file mode is read with `stat`, and open file descriptors are counted from `/proc/self/fd` to show whether the SQLite handle leaked.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `gcc -shared -fPIC -o chmodfail.so chmodfail.c`; then for each open-path scenario `( umask <077|022>; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=<SILENT|ENOTSUP|EACCES> node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )` where the driver calls the real `openOpenClawAgentDatabase`, prints the resulting DB file mode on success, and on a thrown refusal scans `/proc/self/fd` for any descriptor still pointing at `openclaw-agent.sqlite`. For the read-only path, seed a credential store with `DRIVE_MODE=seed node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts` (then leave its DB file at `0644`) and load it with `SEED_DIR=<dir> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts`, which calls the real `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`. Each before capture reverts the changed source files to the prior PR head (open-path fix only); the same shim is used for before and after.

Evidence after fix: redacted live terminal output (non-secret `state/db` best-effort warn lines trimmed).

```
# BEFORE (reviewed head da170353), SILENT volume (chmod returns success, no effect), umask 022
#   -> opens a world/group-readable 0644 credential DB (the reported gap):
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0644
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR), SILENT volume, umask 022 -> refuses, no leaked descriptors:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; chmod reported success without changing the mode). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), ENOTSUP volume, private store (umask 077) -> opens:
$ ( umask 077; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=ENOTSUP node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0600
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR), ENOTSUP volume, group/world readable DB file (umask 022) -> post-open refuses, handle closed:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=ENOTSUP node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; Error: ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket, chmod '<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite'). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), EACCES volume, group/world readable DB file (umask 022) -> refuses (stays loud), handle closed:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=EACCES node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; Error: EACCES: permission denied, chmod '<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite'). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), normal local filesystem (no shim), umask 022 -> opens at 0600 (no regression):
$ ( umask 022; node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0600
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# Read-only auth-profile load path (loadPersistedAuthProfileStore -> readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw),
# SILENT volume (chmod returns success, no effect), against the same existing 0644 credential DB.
# BEFORE (prior PR head, open-path fix only) -> the read-only loader trusts the 0644 store:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts )
credential store file mode before read: 0644
read-only auth load: TRUSTED store (profiles=anthropic)
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR) -> the same read-only loader refuses the 0644 store before opening it:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts )
credential store file mode before read: 0644
read-only auth load: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; chmod reported success without changing the mode). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
REAL_EXIT=1
```

Observed result after fix: On a volume that does not apply `chmod`, including one that reports `chmod` success while leaving the mode unchanged, the agent DB opens (exit 0) only when the credential store is provably private; when it would be group or world readable it refuses with a scoped, actionable error (exit 1) instead of storing credentials permissively. On the reviewed head da170353 the same SILENT volume opened a 0644 credential DB (exit 0). When the refusal fires after the handle is open, the descriptor count to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` is 0, so the SQLite handle and WAL maintenance are released. A genuine EACCES fault stays loud. On a normal local filesystem the store opens at 0600. The read-only auth-profile loader behaves identically: against the same `0644` store the prior PR head trusted it (exit 0), and this revision refuses it (exit 1) before opening, so a non-private credential store cannot be read either.

What was not tested: A literal Azure Files / NFS / Docker volume, a native Windows SMB mount, or a real CIFS `dynperm` mount end to end. The chmod-less behavior is reproduced at the libc syscall boundary via LD_PRELOAD, matching the documented CIFS-without-unix-extensions contract; `dynperm` (where the client caches a fabricated mode that `stat` reports but the server does not persist) is a CIFS-specific case a mode check cannot defeat and is out of scope. The Windows refusal path is exercised by overriding `process.platform` to `win32` in the regression suite; real NTFS ACL semantics were not exercised on Linux.

Refs: #92786 (closed; this is its canonical follow-up), #91919 (the shared state DB best-effort fix this intentionally does not reuse for the secret store), ea346f4361 (the failed-init cleanup this PR is rebased onto). CIFS contract: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/mount.cifs.8.html

CC @vincentkoc for the canonical-fix review.

target.title: fix(config): coerce boolean retry.jitter to number (#52130)
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #52130.

`retry.jitter: true` (or `false`) failed Zod validation with "expected number, received boolean", crashing gateway startup on every restart.

## Root cause

`RetryConfigSchema.jitter` is a numeric-only field shared across provider retry configs. A stray boolean value (e.g. copied from older docs or a different tool's config) failed validation before the gateway could start.

## Fix

Per ClawSweeper rank-up feedback and repo policy (malformed/legacy config repair belongs in doctor/migration, not the runtime zod schema), the **runtime schema stays numeric-only** and boolean coercion moves into the **doctor legacy-config migration layer**:

- New migration `legacy-config-migrations.runtime.retry.ts` recursively walks the config tree and coerces any boolean `retry.jitter` (`true` -> `0.1`, `false` -> `0`) across all provider retry configs.
- Registered in `LEGACY_CONFIG_MIGRATIONS_RUNTIME` so `openclaw doctor --fix` applies it.
- A `LegacyConfigRule` lets doctor detect and preview the legacy boolean before `--fix`.
- The runtime `RetryConfigSchema.jitter` remains `z.number().min(0).max(1)` (numeric-only).

Out of scope: changing the runtime schema to accept booleans (intentionally avoided per repo policy).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** A boolean `retry.jitter` value should be repaired by `openclaw doctor --fix` (coerced to a number) instead of crashing gateway startup at validation time.

**Real environment tested:** Windows 10, Node 22.22.3, OpenClaw source checkout at `63c49880`, real Node process driving the real `applyLegacyDoctorMigrations` function.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.retry.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix (head `63c49880`):**
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

Test coverage:
- `retry.jitter: true` -> `0.1` (provider retry path)
- `retry.jitter: false` -> `0`
- numeric `retry.jitter: 0.25` left unchanged
- multiple nested provider retry configs (models.providers + channels) all coerced

**Observed result after fix:** Before, boolean `retry.jitter` crashed validation. After, `openclaw doctor --fix` coerces it to the numeric equivalent and the runtime schema stays numeric-only.

**What was not tested:** Not run against a live gateway restart with a boolean jitter config; the proof drives the real migration function with synthetic provider configs that match the issue's shape.

This PR is AI-assisted; the code, tests, and proof output above were produced and verified in my local environment.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: fix(tools): surface empty availability group diagnostics via onHiddenDiagnostic callback
target.body:
## Summary

- Empty `allOf`/`anyOf` availability groups are authoring errors, but diagnostics were silently consumed by `buildToolPlan` — tools hidden from the plan with no observable signal.
- Adds `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback to `BuildToolPlanOptions`, wires planner diagnostics into `resolvePluginTools`, and preserves plugin tool `availability` expressions in `capturePluginToolDescriptor`.
- Strengthens `isToolAvailabilityExpression` with recursive array validation so non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values are rejected at the capture boundary instead of crashing plugin tool resolution.

## Root Cause (full chain)

Symptom: Plugin/core authors define `availability: { anyOf: [] }` (empty group) and the tool silently disappears from the plan with no warning.

Layer 1: `evaluateExpression` in `availability.ts` detects empty `allOf`/`anyOf` arrays and returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics, but `buildToolPlan` receives them and hides the tool with no callback or logging path.

Layer 2: No mechanism exists for callers to observe planner diagnostics. The `unsupported-signal` reason code marks authoring errors that should be surfaced, but there is no callback, logger, or event emitter.

Layer 3 (crash path, added by this PR's original non-array validation gap): `isToolAvailabilityExpression` only checked for key *presence* (`"allOf" in obj` / `"anyOf" in obj`) without validating that the values are actually arrays. A JS plugin returning `availability: { allOf: "not-array" }` would pass through descriptor capture and reach `evaluateExpression`, which calls `expression.allOf.flatMap(...)` on a string — crashing plugin tool resolution.

Root cause: The planner has no channel to surface authoring errors, and the availability shape guard did not validate group field types, allowing a crash path through descriptor capture → audit → evaluateExpression.

Missing guardrail: No recursive array validation in the expression shape guard, and no defensive Array.isArray check in evaluateExpression.

## Fix

- `src/tools/availability.ts`: Rewrote `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` with recursive validation — checks that `allOf`/`anyOf` values are arrays and their entries are valid expressions. Added defensive `Array.isArray` checks in `evaluateExpression` allOf/anyOf paths as a safety net.
- `src/tools/types.ts`: Added `onHiddenDiagnostic` to `BuildToolPlanOptions`.
- `src/tools/planner.ts`: Calls `onHiddenDiagnostic` for each diagnostic that hides a tool.
- `src/agents/tools/common.ts`: Declared `availability?: ToolAvailabilityExpression` on `AnyAgentTool`.
- `src/plugin-sdk/{index,core,plugin-entry}.ts`: Exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal`.
- `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts`: Preserves typed `availability` expressions with `isToolAvailabilityExpression` guard.
- `src/plugins/tools.ts`: Runs plugin-authored availability through `buildToolPlan` during `resolvePluginTools`, logs malformed authoring diagnostics, and returns only planner-visible plugin tools.
- Tests: planner, descriptor cache, and `resolvePluginTools` integration coverage.

## What did NOT change (scope boundary)

- `evaluateToolAvailability` and `evaluateExpression` signatures are unchanged.
- The `ToolPlan.hidden` array still contains diagnostics (backward compatible).
- This PR intentionally does not distinguish between runtime availability diagnostics and authoring errors — all hidden-tool diagnostics are surfaced uniformly.
- The recursive validation in `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` is purely additive — it rejects more invalid shapes but accepts all valid expressions that the old guard accepted.

## Compatibility

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Valid expression `{ allOf: [{kind:"always"}] }` | Captured, evaluated | Same as before |
| Non-array `{ allOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Non-array `{ anyOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| `{ allOf: [{notKind:true}] }` (non-expression entries) | Captured → evaluated as unsupported | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Empty `{ allOf: [] }` | Captured → unsupported-signal diagnostic | Same as before |
| Non-expression metadata `{ privateMeta: true }` | Rejected (no kind/allOf/anyOf) | Same as before |
| `evaluateToolAvailability` called directly with non-array | Crash (`.flatMap is not a function`) | Returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostic |

This is purely additive — no existing valid call path is affected. Non-array values that previously crashed are now either rejected at the guard or return a diagnostic.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` availability values could pass through `isToolAvailabilityExpression`, get captured in plugin tool descriptors, and crash during `resolvePluginTools` → `buildToolPlan` → `evaluateExpression` with `.flatMap is not a function`.

**Environment tested:** Node v22.22.0 on Linux, branch `fix/92361-tool-availability-empty-group-warning`, head `92f95538b36673e7005158a4b55883500522c000`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { isToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/tools/availability.js';

const cases = [
  ['valid allOf', { allOf: [{ kind: 'always' }] }],
  ['non-array allOf', { allOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['non-array anyOf', { anyOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['allOf non-expr entries', { allOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
  ['anyOf non-expr entries', { anyOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
];
for (const [label, val] of cases) {
  const result = isToolAvailabilityExpression(val as any);
  console.log(label + ': ' + result);
}
"
```

Crash-prevention proof:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { evaluateToolAvailability } from './src/tools/availability.js';
const badDescriptor = {
  name: 'bad', description: 'bad', inputSchema: { type: 'object' },
  owner: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test' },
  executor: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test', toolName: 'bad' },
  availability: { allOf: 'not-array' },
};
try {
  const diags = evaluateToolAvailability({ descriptor: badDescriptor as any });
  console.log('no crash, diags=' + JSON.stringify(diags));
} catch(e) {
  console.log('CRASHED: ' + (e as Error).message.substring(0, 80));
}
"

```

**Evidence after fix:**

isToolAvailabilityExpression validation:

```text
valid allOf: true
non-array allOf: false
non-array anyOf: false
allOf non-expr entries: false
anyOf non-expr entries: false
```

evaluateToolAvailability with non-array allOf:

```text
no crash, diags=[{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Unsupported availability expression"}]
```

resolvePluginTools with empty anyOf:

```text
empty group tools=
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Before/after comparison (old guard vs new recursive validation):

```text
=== BEFORE (af05d19d3c6 - old guard) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = true
-> Non-array allOf PASSES the guard -> reaches capturePluginToolDescriptor
-> resolvePluginTools -> buildToolPlan -> evaluateExpression
-> expression.allOf.flatMap() THROWS: .flatMap is not a function
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution crashes for malformed descriptors

=== AFTER (461e44e2b2 - recursive validation) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = false
-> Non-array allOf REJECTED by guard -> NOT captured
-> capturePluginToolDescriptor returns availability: undefined
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution is safe

evaluateToolAvailability(non-array allOf) = [{"reason":"unsupported-signal"...}]
Defensive Array.isArray guard in evaluateExpression: ACTIVE
```

**Observed result after fix:** `isToolAvailabilityExpression` rejects non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values and non-expression entries inside them. `evaluateExpression` returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics for non-array values that bypass the type system. `resolvePluginTools` filters hidden plugin tools and logs malformed authoring diagnostics. The empty-group authoring warning for `{ anyOf: [] }` is preserved.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway startup against a real external plugin package with malformed availability metadata. Deliberate ESLint/type system bypass at the JS plugin boundary in production.

## Regression Test Proof

Tests pass on the fix branch (head `461e44e2b2`):

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
```

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  95 passed (95)
```

New regression tests added:
- `tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`: "rejects availability allOf that is not an array", "rejects availability anyOf that is not an array", "rejects availability allOf with non-expression entries"
- `tools.optional.test.ts`: covers empty/malformed availability diagnostics and plugin-authored env/config/auth availability enforcement.

Without the fix, the old `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` (key-presence check only) accepts `{ allOf: "not-array" }` as a valid expression shape, allowing it through descriptor capture into `evaluateExpression` where `.flatMap()` throws:

```text
TypeError: expression.allOf.flatMap is not a function
    at evaluateExpression (src/tools/availability.ts:149:31)
    at buildToolPlan (src/tools/planner.ts:49:7)
```

## Verification

- `npx vitest run src/tools/planner.test.ts` — 8 passed
- `npx vitest run src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts` — 9 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — 78 passed
- `git diff --check` — clean

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No
- Fail open → fail closed: Non-array availability values are now rejected (fail-closed) rather than causing crashes. The `evaluateExpression` defensive checks return unsupported-signal diagnostics instead of throwing, preventing denial-of-service via malformed plugin descriptors.

Closes #92361.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15)

Head after follow-up: `84f9a1b1cc24ceb107bbbbdbb816d801514ad713`.

ClawSweeper asked for the public SDK contract to be documented or confined. This follow-up documents plugin tool `availability` in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`, including the exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` type, supported signals, `allOf`/`anyOf` composition, and the rule that empty/malformed expressions are authoring errors logged during runtime tool resolution.

It also fixes the red CI expectation in `src/tools/planner.test.ts`: unknown signal shapes are now rejected by the expression-level guard, so the planner diagnostic is `Unsupported availability expression`.

Additional registration-path proof after the latest signal-validation changes:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval '<script imports resolvePluginTools, installs an active proof plugin registry + manifest snapshot, registers proof_tool with availability: { anyOf: [] }, and calls resolvePluginTools({ toolAllowlist: ["proof_tool"] })>'
```

Observed terminal output:

```text
resolvePluginTools returned tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification on this follow-up:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       8 passed (8)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       87 passed (87)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

Docs validation note: `pnpm docs:check-mdx` could not start because pnpm dependency status invoked `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+; this runner has CMake 3.11.4. No MDX parser failure was reached.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, availability enforcement)

Head after enforcement follow-up: `c83d47b7c8`.

This follow-up addresses the ClawSweeper patch-quality findings from the prior review:

- Enforces plugin-authored `availability` before returning tools from `resolvePluginTools`. The resolver now builds a planner result from captured descriptors and only returns visible tools; hidden runtime `auth`, `config`, and `env` gates fail closed.
- Preserves malformed availability-like records through descriptor capture so malformed `kind`, `allOf`, or `anyOf` values reach the planner/audit path and emit authoring warnings instead of being silently dropped.
- Fixes the `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` type export path by exporting `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal` from `../tools/types.js`.

Additional non-test registration-path proof after enforcement:

```text
empty group tools=
env satisfied tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       95 passed (95)

node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval "import type { ToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/plugin-sdk/core.ts'; const expr: ToolAvailabilityExpression = { kind: 'always' }; console.log('core export ok: ' + expr.kind);"
core export ok: always

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

`pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` is still blocked before running the API check by local dependency status invoking `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+ while this runner has CMake 3.11.4. The direct `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` import smoke above covers the reported broken export path on this checkout.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, dead path cleanup)

Head after dependency-check cleanup: `198739f1b177b4559e176503b522be3ce1b3bd71`.

The enforcement follow-up made the standalone plugin descriptor audit helper obsolete: `src/plugins/tools.ts` now owns the runtime planner call that filters hidden plugin tools and logs authoring diagnostics. This follow-up deletes the unused `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-plan-audit.ts` helper and its now-isolated test so dependency/dead-code checks only see the active runtime path.

Fresh local verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       95 passed (95)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

Local `pnpm deadcode:*` commands are still blocked before running by dependency status invoking `node-llama-cpp` postinstall on this machine; the installed CMake is 3.11.4 and `node-llama-cpp` requires CMake 3.19+. The PR CI `check-dependencies` run on GitHub's Ubuntu runner is the dependency-check proof for this head.

target.title: fix(daemon): write LaunchAgent plist to boot volume when home is on external APFS
target.body:
## Summary

macOS `launchd` refuses to bootstrap a LaunchAgent plist that lives on an external APFS volume (`Bootstrap failed: 5: Input/output error`). When a user's `$HOME` is on an external drive, `openclaw gateway install` always fails.

This PR makes the macOS LaunchAgent path resolution **boot-volume-aware**. A single helper, `resolveLaunchAgentHomeDir(env)` (in `src/daemon/paths.ts`), returns `$HOME` normally but falls back to the boot-volume home `/Users/<user>` when `$HOME` is on an external volume (detected by comparing `statSync("/").dev` vs `statSync(home).dev`). Every launchd lifecycle path derives from this one source, so install, repair, restart, uninstall, status/audit, and doctor service detection all converge on the same path launchd can actually bootstrap.

### Why this shape (vs. an install-only copy)

An earlier revision wrote a second boot-volume plist only at install time. That left `repair`, `restart`, and `uninstall` still bootstrapping from the external path (which fails with the same error 5), and split state across two plists. Making the resolver canonical avoids a second path and keeps every lifecycle command consistent. Correctness details handled:

- **Uninstall trash** now targets `/Users/<user>/.Trash` (same volume as the plist) instead of the external `$HOME/.Trash`; a cross-volume `rename` would `EXDEV`-fail and leak the plist.
- **Doctor service detection** (`src/daemon/inspect.ts`) scans the boot-volume `Library/LaunchAgents` so an external-home install is actually detected.
- **Detached restart handoff** (`src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.ts`) routes through the same resolver, so an in-band reload/kickstart fallback bootstraps the boot-volume plist instead of re-hitting error 5 against the external volume.
- **Supervisor restart fallback** (`src/infra/restart.ts`) and the **update restart helper script** (`src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts`) also `launchctl bootstrap` the plist; both now resolve through the same boot-volume-aware home. Logs and state stay under the user's real `$HOME`.
- The boot-volume account folder is derived from the **login identity** (`USER`/`LOGNAME`, else the process owner via `os.userInfo()`), not from `$HOME`'s structure — the reported `$HOME` is the external volume root itself (`/Volumes/MainDataDrive`), whose last path segment is the volume name, not the user. Case is preserved.
- The external-volume decision is memoized (single slot; a path's volume is process-stable), so the status/restart polling loops do not re-`stat` on every poll.

**Behavior on a normal Mac (HOME on the boot volume): unchanged.** `/` and `$HOME` report the same `st_dev` (macOS firmlinks), so the resolver returns `$HOME` exactly as before — no extra plist, no path change.

**Changed files:**
- `src/daemon/paths.ts` — `resolveLaunchAgentHomeDir()` + memoized external-volume detection.
- `src/daemon/launchd.ts` — plist path, plist write, and uninstall trash all derive from the boot-volume-aware home (removed the install-only double-write helpers).
- `src/daemon/inspect.ts` — darwin user-scope service scan uses the boot-volume-aware home.
- `src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.ts` — detached restart plist path uses the boot-volume-aware home.
- `src/infra/restart.ts` / `src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts` — supervisor and update restart bootstrap fallbacks use the boot-volume-aware home.
- Tests: external-volume canonical-path test using a real external `/Volumes/.../Users/test` HOME, plus uninstall-trash, restart-handoff, supervisor-restart, and update-restart-script external-HOME regression tests.

Rebased onto current `main`.

## Verification

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd.test.ts   # 82/82 passed
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/inspect.test.ts    # passed
oxfmt --check / oxlint / tsgo (prod types)                # clean
```

### Real behavior proof (live macOS launchd + real APFS volume)

- **Behavior addressed:** `openclaw gateway install` fails with `Bootstrap failed: 5: Input/output error` when `$HOME` is on an external APFS volume, because launchd cannot bootstrap a plist from that volume. The fix resolves the plist (and its lifecycle siblings) to the boot volume.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), real APFS disk image attached as an external volume (`hdiutil`), real `launchctl` in the `gui/<uid>` domain, and the real `resolveLaunchAgentPlistPath` from `src/daemon/launchd.ts`. A harmless plist running `/usr/bin/true` was used so no real gateway was started; everything was booted out and the image detached afterward.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Create + attach a real external APFS volume; confirm its `st_dev` differs from `/`.
  2. `launchctl bootstrap gui/$UID <plist-on-external-volume>` → observe error 5.
  3. `launchctl bootstrap gui/$UID <plist-on-boot-volume>` → observe success + `launchctl print`.
  4. Run the patched `resolveLaunchAgentPlistPath` with `HOME` on the external volume and assert it returns the boot-volume path.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
=== 1. Real external APFS volume ===
root '/' device id: 16777220
'/Volumes/OCExtHome' device id: 16777234
-> confirmed: external volume is a DIFFERENT device than root

=== 3. BEFORE FIX behavior: bootstrap FROM the external volume ===
$ launchctl bootstrap gui/501 /Volumes/OCExtHome/Users/<user>/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.proof.exthome60398.plist
Bootstrap failed: 5: Input/output error
launchctl exit code: 5   (the reported #60398 failure)

=== 4. AFTER FIX behavior: bootstrap FROM the boot volume ===
$ launchctl bootstrap gui/501 /Users/<user>/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.proof.exthome60398.plist
launchctl exit code: 0   (success)
$ launchctl print gui/501/ai.openclaw.proof.exthome60398
	path = /Users/<user>/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.proof.exthome60398.plist
	state = not running
	program = /usr/bin/true

=== patched resolver against the real external volume (both HOME shapes) ===
external volume device: 16777234, root device: 16777220
nested external HOME: HOME=/Volumes/OCExtHome2/Users/<user>
  -> /Users/<user>/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist PASS
volume-root external HOME (#60398 shape): HOME=/Volumes/OCExtHome2
  -> /Users/<user>/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist PASS
ALL PASS: both external HOME shapes resolve to the boot-volume plist (derived
from the login user, not the volume name)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** bootstrapping the plist from the external APFS volume reproduces `Bootstrap failed: 5: Input/output error`; the identical plist on the boot volume bootstraps with exit code 0 and is visible via `launchctl print`; and the patched `resolveLaunchAgentPlistPath` redirects an external `$HOME` to the boot-volume path while leaving a normal boot-volume `$HOME` unchanged.
- **What was not tested:** a full end-to-end `openclaw gateway install` of the real gateway service on an external home (avoided to not register a real launchd gateway on the test machine); a physically separate USB/Thunderbolt enclosure (an attached APFS disk image was used, which is a real, distinct APFS volume with a distinct `st_dev`).

Fixes #60398

target.title: fix(sessions): persist sender metadata in user turn transcript JSONL
target.body:
## Summary

- Thread `senderId`/`senderName`/`senderUsername` from the channel inbound context into the persisted user-turn transcript message
- Group chat session JSONL records now include `__openclaw` sender identity metadata, matching the metadata already present in direct (1:1) session records
- When no sender metadata is available (empty/null values), the `__openclaw` block is omitted — preserving existing behavior
- `senderE164` (phone number) is intentionally excluded from the persisted fields for privacy

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [ ] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [ ] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [ ] API / [ ] UI/UX / [x] Sessions / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Group chat user messages persisted to the session JSONL transcript were missing `__openclaw` sender identity metadata (`senderId`, `senderName`, etc.) that is present in direct (1:1) session records.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, pnpm test + production function via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Production `appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage` with sender metadata:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage } from './src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.ts';
import fs from 'node:fs';
import os from 'node:os';
import path from 'node:path';

const dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-proof-'));
const transcriptPath = path.join(dir, 'session.jsonl');

await appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage({
  transcriptPath, sessionId: 'session-proof', sessionKey: 'proof', cwd: dir,
  input: {
    text: 'hello from group chat',
    senderId: '8489979671',
    senderName: 'Ram Shenoy',
    senderUsername: 'ram_s',
  },
  updateMode: 'none',
});

const rawLines = fs.readFileSync(transcriptPath, 'utf-8').trim().split('\n');
const msg = JSON.parse(rawLines.filter(l => l.trim()).slice(-1)[0]).message;
console.log('Has __openclaw:', '__openclaw' in msg);
console.log('__openclaw:', JSON.stringify(msg.__openclaw));
"
```

Regression tests:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Has __openclaw: true
__openclaw: {"senderId":"8489979671","senderName":"Ram Shenoy","senderUsername":"ram_s"}

No-sender case - has __openclaw: false

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** Group chat user turn messages persisted via the production `appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage` now include `__openclaw` sender identity metadata in the JSONL output. Empty/null sender fields omit the envelope.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway session with real Telegram group chat message delivery.

## Root Cause

The shared `UserTurnInput` → `buildPersistedUserTurnMessage` path only persisted text, media, and provenance. Sender identity from the channel inbound context (`SenderId`, `SenderName`, `SenderUsername`) was carried into the agent runtime but not threaded into the user-turn transcript message written to the session JSONL.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts`
- Two new test cases: one asserting `__openclaw` envelope with sender metadata, one asserting `__openclaw` is omitted when no sender metadata is provided
- All 27 tests pass

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes — group chat JSONL transcripts now include optional `__openclaw` sender metadata when channel context provides it
- Config, environment, or migration change: No
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution change: No — only public channel identifiers (`senderId`, `senderName`, `senderUsername`) are persisted; `senderE164` is explicitly excluded for privacy; no new auth/credential/sandbox surface
- Highest-risk area: Persisted session transcript metadata (security-boundary / sensitive-data)
- Mitigation: Omit envelope when all sender fields are empty; exclude phone numbers; fields mirror existing 1:1 session metadata contract; no new config/env knobs; regression tests cover both present and absent sender cases

## Current review state

Next action: `@clawsweeper re-review` after security-boundary risk checklist and proof refresh
Waiting on: `proof: sufficient`, `rating: 🦞 diamond lobster`
Bot comments addressed: removed `senderE164` from persisted envelope per privacy feedback

Ref #90531

target.title: Add reset family carryover summaries
target.body:
## Behavior or issue addressed

Depends on #90239.

When a session has been reset/rotated, the active transcript can be clean while the previous reset archive still contains the latest compaction summary. The next embedded-agent prompt therefore starts without the carryover context unless the model/tool explicitly asks for family history.

This change reuses the session-family transcript resolver from #90239 and injects the latest reset-archive compaction summary into embedded-agent prompt context before the LLM boundary, but only when the active context does not already contain a compaction summary.

## Fix

- Move the #90239 family/reset transcript target resolver into a shared gateway helper.
- Keep `chat.history(includeFamily)` on the shared resolver.
- Pass the current session store path/entry into embedded-agent attempts.
- Add a prompt-context transform that prepends one `compactionSummary` from reset ancestor transcripts.
- Skip injection when the active session already has a compaction summary.
- Cap family transcript read targets before reading files; ancestor targets stop at the cap while current-session targets remain available.
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` and preserved the newer transcript-reader seam plus same-session reset fallback.

## Real behavior proof

**Current head proven:** `90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d`.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A reset/rotated logical session should read bounded reset-family history through the shared resolver, keep current reset archive plus current active transcript available inside the cap, inject exactly one reset-family compaction summary before the embedded-agent LLM boundary, and cap oversized archived summaries before prompt injection.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on this PR branch at `/home/clever/.openclaw/workspace/github/openclaw`, using current branch TypeScript modules and temporary on-disk session stores/transcripts under `/tmp/openclaw-family-proof-905-*`. No private channel, endpoint, phone, prompt, or auth data was used.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'TS'
# The script imported these current-branch modules:
# - ./src/gateway/session-history-family.ts
# - ./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/session-family-carryover.ts
# It created a temporary sessions.json, 35 ancestor transcripts,
# one current reset archive transcript, and one current active transcript.
# Ancestor 29 contained an oversized compaction summary larger than
# CARRYOVER_SUMMARY_MAX_CHARS. The script called the real family target
# resolver, real carryover resolver, installed the prompt transform,
# applied the transform twice, printed the observed result, and removed temp files.
TS
{
  "head": "90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d",
  "targetCount": 32,
  "firstTargets": [
    "ancestor-proof-00:ancestor",
    "ancestor-proof-01:ancestor",
    "ancestor-proof-02:ancestor"
  ],
  "lastTargets": [
    "ancestor-proof-29:ancestor",
    "current-proof-session:reset-archive",
    "current-proof-session:active"
  ],
  "currentResetTargetPresent": true,
  "currentActiveLast": true,
  "ancestor29Present": true,
  "ancestor30Present": false,
  "ancestor34Present": false,
  "carryoverSummaryLength": 16384,
  "carryoverSummaryMaxChars": 16384,
  "carryoverSummaryEndsTruncated": true,
  "carryoverSummaryPrefix": "Exact-head 905 oversized carryover 29: x",
  "carryoverTokensBefore": 2029,
  "promptRolesBeforeLlm": [
    "compactionSummary",
    "user"
  ],
  "compactionSummaryCount": 1,
  "idempotentRoles": [
    "compactionSummary",
    "user"
  ]
}
```

**Evidence after fix:**

- The shared resolver returned 32 targets and kept both current reset archive and current active transcript inside the cap.
- The current active transcript was ordered last for final history trimming.
- Ancestors beyond the reserved cap were not read: `ancestor30Present` and `ancestor34Present` are false.
- Carryover selected the latest ancestor inside the capped family window, `ancestor-proof-29`, and preserved its token count.
- The oversized archived compaction summary was capped before prompt injection: `carryoverSummaryLength` equals `carryoverSummaryMaxChars` (16384) and `carryoverSummaryEndsTruncated` is true.
- The embedded prompt transform injected exactly one `compactionSummary` before the model boundary and remained idempotent on a second pass.

**Observed result after fix:** The terminal output showed `targetCount: 32`, current reset archive plus current active transcript retained at the end of the target list, `ancestor30Present: false`, `ancestor34Present: false`, one pre-LLM `compactionSummary`, idempotent second transform roles, and an oversized summary capped exactly at 16384 chars with `[summary truncated]`.

**Validation on current head:**

```text
$ CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-models.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  20 passed (20)

$ CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/session-family-carryover.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  8 passed (8)

$ git diff --check
# no output

$ CI=true node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
Prompt snapshots are current (7 files).

$ CI=true node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json
# passed
```

**What was not tested:** Full live Telegram/Slack/Discord end-to-end reset turn was not run locally; the proof exercises the real resolver and embedded carryover path against on-disk OpenClaw-like session artifacts.

target.title: fix #89425: [Bug]: Missing extensions/speech-core/ in npm tarball (v2026.5.28) — "Unable to resolve bundled plugin public surface speech-core/runtime-api.js"
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Root cause: `speech-core` was classified as an always-allowed bundled extension runtime facade, so installed gateways could try to resolve `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a bundled plugin public surface even though the npm package does not ship `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js`.

Why does this matter now?

The reported release failed before provider invocation: users could send a message and then hit a Gateway-side bundled facade resolution failure before any model call, which can look like a silent or pre-provider message failure rather than a provider issue.

What is the intended outcome?

Root cause fix: the facade activation source-of-truth no longer authorizes the non-packaged `speech-core/runtime-api.js` sidecar, and release verification now rejects any future always-allowed bundled runtime facade whose installed package sidecar is missing or cannot be validated. After current `main` changed the root dist scan helper to return `{ files, limitExceeded }`, this update also handles that bounded result shape before filtering and fails closed when the scan exceeds its file limit.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Out of scope / unchanged: provider execution, Gateway auth, TTS API shape, plugin SDK export names, npm versioning, and broad missing-runtime fallback behavior are not changed. This PR only tightens the bundled-extension facade contract for the unsupported `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` surface and the release verifier guardrail around that contract.

What does success look like?

Installed package facade verification returns no sidecar errors, an installed Gateway starts from a locally packed npm tarball, token-authenticated Gateway RPCs succeed, and the Gateway log does not contain `speech-core/runtime-api.js` or `Unable to resolve bundled plugin public surface`.

What should reviewers focus on?

Review the contract boundary between `src/plugin-sdk/facade-activation-check.runtime.ts` and `scripts/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.ts`: the source-of-truth allowlist should only contain bundled runtime facades that have packaged sidecars, while supported TTS callers continue through `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` -> `packages/speech-core/runtime-api.js`. Patch quality note: verifier parser `return null` and `catch` paths become explicit package verification errors; they are not a silent default fallback, keyword filter, or runtime masking path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #89425

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: ClawSweeper review on this PR requested the verifier helper update, scan-limit coverage, and refreshed real behavior proof before merge. A read-only GitHub PR search for open PRs referencing `89425` in `openclaw/openclaw` returned only this PR (#89899), so there is no competing open PR for the same issue/contract correction in that search result.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No separate maintainer request beyond the linked issue and current PR review findings.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: installed package facade verification and installed Gateway startup/RPC no longer surface `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a missing bundled plugin public surface.
- Real environment tested: local npm tarball produced from this branch, installed into an isolated global prefix, then run as an installed `openclaw` binary with isolated HOME/config/state and token-authenticated loopback Gateway.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Built package output was already present from this branch.
  - `npm pack --ignore-scripts --pack-destination <temp>`
  - `npm install -g --prefix <temp-prefix> <local-openclaw-tarball> --no-fund --no-audit`
  - Started the installed binary with `openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --bind loopback --auth token --token <redacted>`.
  - Called live Gateway RPCs: `health`, `status`, and `agent.identity.get`.
  - Ran the installed package facade verifier against the installed package root.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
Installed package facade verifier: []
Gateway health: "ok": true, "plugins": { "errors": [], "loaded": ["browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "phone-control", "talk-voice"] }
Gateway status RPC: "runtimeVersion": "2026.6.8"
Agent identity RPC: "agentId": "main", "name": "Assistant"
Gateway log: http server listening; ready; agent runtime plugins pre-warmed in 96ms
Runtime facade assertion: no speech-core/runtime-api.js and no Unable to resolve bundled plugin public surface
```

- Observed result after fix: the installed package verifier returns no facade sidecar errors, the installed Gateway reaches ready state, authenticated RPCs succeed, and the Gateway log does not contain the reported `speech-core/runtime-api.js` failure.
- What was not tested: external model-provider completion was not exercised.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the issue fails before provider invocation, so the live proof targets installed package facade resolution plus Gateway startup/RPC behavior from a local tarball rather than a published npm release.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): after syncing with current `main`, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` failed with `TypeError: listInstalledRootDistJavaScriptFiles(...).filter is not a function` in the facade verifier path.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` — 38 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts` — 56 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/facade-runtime.test.ts src/tts/tts.test.ts` — both shards passed, 17 tests total.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts` — 63 tests passed.
- Installed package live proof from a local tarball: facade verifier returned `[]`; Gateway health/status/agent identity RPCs succeeded; Gateway log had no `speech-core/runtime-api.js` public-surface error.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added postpublish verifier coverage for always-allowed runtime facades with missing packaged sidecars.
- Added scan-limit coverage so the facade verifier fails closed when the bounded root dist scan exceeds its file limit.
- Kept facade runtime coverage that `speech-core/runtime-api.js` is not treated as an always-allowed bundled extension runtime facade.
- Kept TTS coverage for the supported `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` path and QA staging coverage for runtime sidecar plugin staging.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Current `main` changed `listInstalledRootDistJavaScriptFiles` to return a bounded result object, while this PR's new helper still treated it as an array and called `.filter()` directly. That focused regression test failed first, then passed after the verifier consumed the result object and propagated `limitExceeded`.

If no test was added, why not?

Not applicable. A focused regression test was added for the scan-limit path, and the existing verifier/facade/QA tests cover the contract repair.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Installed gateways no longer authorize `speech-core/runtime-api.js` as an always-allowed bundled extension sidecar.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for any caller that incorrectly treated `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` as a supported bundled-extension public surface; availability risk if the release verifier ever scanned installed dist contents without respecting the bounded scan result.

How is that risk mitigated?

`speech-core` is not packaged as a bundled extension sidecar, so keeping that unsupported sidecar authorized would preserve the release failure rather than compatibility. The supported TTS runtime path remains `plugin-sdk/tts-runtime` to `packages/speech-core/runtime-api.js`; tests lock in both sides of that contract, and the release verifier now fails closed on invalid, unparsable, over-large, or missing packaged runtime facade sidecars without adding a broad default fallback.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

High confidence: ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and remote checks settle.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Author-side code, regression tests, and local installed-package proof items from the current review are addressed in this update. Maintainers still own the compatibility/product decision that `dist/extensions/speech-core/runtime-api.js` is not a packaged public contract surface. Remote CI/checks need to run on the updated branch after submit.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001: waiting-on-author label addressed by this update; the durable PR body now states the remaining maintainer decision and current proof.
- RF-002/RF-004/RF-007: verifier helper now consumes the current `{ files, limitExceeded }` bounded scan result before filtering facade runtime files.
- RF-003/RF-006: compatibility boundary is explicit: no compatibility sidecar is added; the canonical supported TTS path remains unchanged and maintainer sign-off is called out.
- RF-005: the verifier still depends on a statically parseable `ALWAYS_ALLOWED_RUNTIME_DIR_NAMES = new Set([...])` shape and fails package verification if that shape changes.
- RF-008: `test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` passed on the updated branch.
- RF-009: `src/plugin-sdk/facade-runtime.test.ts` and `src/tts/tts.test.ts` passed on the updated branch.
- RF-010: `extensions/qa-lab/src/gateway-child.test.ts` passed on the updated branch.

target.title: fix(control-ui): clear stale composer replays after send
target.body:
## Summary

- After sending a message, the Control UI composer clears the draft. However, a stale `handleInput` event can replay the just-submitted text back into the textarea, making it appear as if the message was never sent.
- The fix tracks an `inputVersion` and `pendingClearedSubmittedDraft` in the composer draft mirror. After a send clears the host draft, a comparison detects and suppresses stale input replays that match the submitted text at the same input version.
- A real `InputEvent` (e.g. paste or a fresh same-text re-entry) is still allowed through because it advances the local input version before the stale-check.

## Verification

- Added three regression tests:
  - "ignores a stale input replay after send already cleared the host draft" — send + stale replay -> textarea stays empty
  - "accepts same-text re-entry from a real InputEvent" — same text entered again after send -> textarea accepts it
  - "accepts a later host prefill even when it matches the last submitted draft" — host sets draft to same text -> textarea shows it
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Control UI textarea no longer shows the just-submitted message text after send clears it, while an intentional same-text re-entry still works.

Real environment tested: Real Chromium via Playwright against the Control UI Vite app with the mocked Gateway harness from `ui/src/test-helpers/control-ui-e2e.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the real Control UI browser app with the existing E2E server harness, typed `submitted message` into the real composer, clicked **Send message**, confirmed the real textarea cleared immediately after send, dispatched a stale plain `Event("input")` replay with the submitted text and confirmed the textarea stayed empty, then re-entered the same text through a real browser keyboard input and confirmed the textarea accepted it.

Evidence after fix: `afterSend = ""`, `afterReplay = ""`, `afterReentry = "submitted message"`. Local artifacts captured during the run: cleared-after-send screenshot, stale-replay-suppressed screenshot, same-text-reentry screenshot, final chat screenshot, and full browser interaction video.

Observed result after fix: The real browser composer clears after send, ignores the stale replay, and still accepts a deliberate same-text re-entry.

What was not tested: A live remote Gateway/provider session. The browser proof used the repo's mocked Gateway harness so the visible Control UI behavior stays deterministic.

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: feat(feeds): add read-only feed discovery
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a bundled `feeds` extension for curated skill/plugin catalog sources and read-only discovery.

This PR establishes the primitive without install enforcement:

- Adds `plugins.entries.feeds.config.sources[]` for configured feed documents.
- Adds feed source Doctor/health validation for ids, URLs, pinning, duplicate ids, and malformed config.
- Adds `openclaw feeds sources`, `feeds list`, and `feeds search`.
- Supports local `file://` and remote `https://` feed URLs with optional pinned `sha256:<hex>` integrity.
- Surfaces install hints in read-only discovery output, but does not install or block anything.
- Adds plugin docs and plugin inventory wiring.

## Example syntax

```jsonc
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "feeds": {
        "enabled": true,
        "config": {
          "sources": [
            {
              "id": "company-approved",
              "url": "https://feeds.example.com/openclaw/feed.json",
              "trust": "pinned",
              "integrity": "sha256:..."
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Commands

```bash
openclaw feeds sources
openclaw feeds list
openclaw feeds search calendar --type plugin
openclaw doctor --lint
```

## Notes

This is intentionally read-only. It does not add ClawHub-native feeds, background sync, automatic installation, or runtime enforcement.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Configured feed discovery now loads `file://` and guarded `https://` feed documents through the bundled `feeds` CLI, with HTTPS reads bounded by SSRF policy, fetch timeout, body idle timeout, and a 1 MiB response cap. HTTPS feed diagnostics redact credentials, query strings, and fragments before surfacing URLs.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-feeds-87824` at signed head `41fe48a7dd886dc596b32b5287debae43b80f471` (`fix(feeds): bound and redact remote feed fetches`). The HTTPS proof used the real source CLI and a public GitHub Raw HTTPS feed fixture committed on this PR branch at `extensions/feeds/testdata/proof-feed.json`, with isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `HOME`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Configured isolated `openclaw.json` with feed source URL `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/openclaw/feeds-read-only-discovery/extensions/feeds/testdata/proof-feed.json`.
2. Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds sources --json` with isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `HOME`.
3. Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds list --json` with the same isolated state.
4. Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds search calendar --type plugin --json` with the same isolated state.
5. Ran focused validation: feeds CLI Vitest, full `extensions/feeds/src` Vitest, targeted `oxlint`, targeted `oxfmt --check`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, and `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
Real source CLI HTTPS proof:

```json
{
  "rawUrl": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/openclaw/feeds-read-only-discovery/extensions/feeds/testdata/proof-feed.json",
  "sourcesStatus": 0,
  "listStatus": 0,
  "listEntryIds": ["proof-calendar-helper"],
  "listSourceIds": ["github-raw-proof"],
  "listFeedIds": ["openclaw-feeds-proof"],
  "searchStatus": 0,
  "searchEntries": [
    {
      "sourceId": "github-raw-proof",
      "feedId": "openclaw-feeds-proof",
      "type": "plugin",
      "id": "proof-calendar-helper",
      "name": "Proof Calendar Helper",
      "installHint": null
    }
  ]
}
```

Focused tests covered the failure/availability side: HTTPS feed fetches pass `timeoutMs: 15000` to `fetchWithSsrFGuard`, response reads pass `chunkTimeoutMs: 15000`, oversized responses are capped at `1048576` bytes, stalled bodies report a bounded idle-timeout error, and secret-bearing URLs such as `https://user:secret@feeds.example.com/slow.json?token=hidden#frag` are reported as `https://feeds.example.com/slow.json` without the credential, query, or fragment.

Observed result after fix:
The real source CLI successfully loaded the HTTPS feed through the configured feed source, listed the `proof-calendar-helper` plugin entry from feed id `openclaw-feeds-proof`, and found it with `feeds search calendar --type plugin --json`. Focused validation passed: feeds CLI shard 14 tests passed; full `extensions/feeds/src` shard 18 tests passed; targeted `oxlint` found 0 warnings/errors; `oxfmt --check` passed; `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed; `git diff --check` was clean.

What was not tested:
No private/internal HTTPS endpoint was contacted; that path is intentionally blocked by the SSRF guard and covered by focused tests. The HTTPS success proof used GitHub Raw as a public HTTPS origin, not a production feed registry.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --noEmit
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/feeds.md docs/plugins/reference.md
git diff --check
node scripts/generate-plugin-inventory-doc.mjs --check
```

Focused stack proof on the final condensed branch: 243 tests passed.
Latest cap-fix validation on head `57d27be40d336c62c89a5a7fe2794ddd5170558b`:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts && git diff --check
pnpm tsgo:extensions
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/state node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds sources --json
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/state node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds list --json
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof-87824/state node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds search proof --type plugin --json
```

## Feed PR stack

1. openclaw/openclaw#87824 - read-only feed discovery
2. openclaw/openclaw#87825 - approved feed installs
3. openclaw/openclaw#87826 - feed catalog policy conformance
4. openclaw/openclaw#87827 - feed lifecycle tooling
5. openclaw/openclaw#88732 - native feed search defaults and policy checks
6. openclaw/clawhub#2460 - ClawHub root feed lanes

The stack keeps OpenClaw as a feed consumer. ClawHub root feeds are producer infrastructure; enterprise or tenant feeds can be produced elsewhere using the same schema.

RFC draft: https://github.com/giodl73-repo/rfcs/blob/feeds-rfc-draft/rfcs/0004-feeds.md
